title: Superforecasting
subtitle: The Art and Science of Prediction
author: Philip E. Tetlock & Dan Gardner
tagline: Learn the measurable habits that let ordinary people out-predict CIA analysts.
whyRead: Every plan you make rests on a forecast, yet almost nobody checks whether their predictions come true. Tetlock spent decades scoring thousands of forecasts and found a small group who reliably beat intelligence professionals — using learnable habits, not genius. This book is the manual for those habits. After reading it you will break vague questions into estimable parts, state beliefs as precise probabilities, update them without lurching, and know exactly how good your judgment really is.

map.intro:
Most expert prediction is never scored, so bad forecasters keep their reputations and nobody improves. Tetlock's core argument: forecasting is a skill, it can be measured, and once measured it can be trained. His 20-year **Expert Political Judgment** study made headlines by showing the average expert did about as well as random guessing — but the overlooked finding was that one style of thinking consistently beat another. When the U.S. intelligence community funded a massive forecasting tournament after the Iraq WMD fiasco, Tetlock's volunteers — retired programmers, homemakers, film-makers — beat professional analysts with access to classified information.

The book unfolds in three movements. Chapters 1–2 make the case that judgment must be kept score of, and introduce the tournaments and the Brier score that make scoring possible. Chapters 3–6 dissect how the top 2% — **superforecasters** — actually think: they decompose questions, start from base rates, express beliefs in fine-grained probabilities, update relentlessly in small steps, and treat their own ability as a work in progress. Chapters 7–8 scale the lessons up: how teams amplify or destroy accuracy, why leaders can be both decisive and humble, and what Taleb's black-swan objection does and does not defeat. The book closes with a compact set of commandments you can start practicing on your next real decision.
map.howToUse: Chapters 1–2 set up the evidence; skim them if you already trust the premise. Chapters 3–5 are the essential core — the actual thinking techniques. Chapter 6 matters if you want to keep improving; Chapter 7 if you forecast in teams or run meetings; Chapter 8 for leadership and the honest limits of prediction.

chapters.title: The Dart-Throwing Chimp and the Illusion of Knowledge
chapters.summary:
Tetlock's famous 20-year study — 284 experts, roughly 28,000 predictions — found the average pundit was about as accurate as a **dart-throwing chimp**, a soundbite that obscured the real result: some forecasters were meaningfully better than chance, especially on shorter horizons. The lesson is not that prediction is hopeless but that it is unmeasured. Pundits like Tom Friedman are never scored, so their accuracy is unknowable in principle.

Medicine is the cautionary parallel. For centuries confident physicians killed patients because nothing was tested; only when **randomized controlled trials** (championed by Archie Cochrane) forced measurement did medicine start improving. Forecasting today is pre-Cochrane medicine.

Why do smart people misjudge? Kahneman's **System 1** delivers instant, coherent-feeling answers from the **tip-of-your-nose perspective**, and we rarely engage deliberate **System 2** checking. **Attribute substitution** quietly swaps a hard question for an easy one, and **confirmation bias** protects the first answer. Yet computers cannot replace human judgment in messy domains — so the goal is to keep score, find what works, and train it. That optimistic skepticism frames the whole book.
readIf: You quote expert predictions without knowing their track records
You trust your gut but have never tested it
You want the evidence before adopting the method

chapters.title: Keeping Score: Foxes, Hedgehogs, and the Tournament
chapters.summary:
You cannot judge forecasts like 'Russia will probably escalate' — vague words and elastic timelines make everyone right in hindsight. The fix is precise questions, explicit probabilities, and the **Brier score**, which measures the gap between forecast and outcome (0 is perfect, 0.5 is chance-level guessing, 2.0 is perfectly wrong). Good scoring must respect **calibration** (your 70% events happen 70% of the time) and **resolution** (daring to say 90% rather than hedging at 55%).

Scored this way, Tetlock's EPJ data split experts into Isaiah Berlin's types: **hedgehogs**, who force events into One Big Idea, and **foxes**, who stitch together many partial views. Foxes clearly won; hedgehogs did worse the more famous and confident they were — media appeal correlates inversely with accuracy.

After the Iraq WMD failure, IARPA launched a forecasting tournament in 2011. Tetlock's **Good Judgment Project**, staffed by thousands of ordinary volunteers, beat rival university teams by 30–70% and reportedly out-predicted intelligence analysts with classified access by about 30%. The top 2% — superforecasters — were not lucky: most stayed at the top year after year, defying regression to the mean.
readIf: You make claims like 'this will probably work' and never check
You want to know how accuracy is actually measured
You suspect the loudest voice in the room is the least reliable

chapters.title: How Superforecasters Think: Fermi-ize and Zoom Out
chapters.summary:
Superforecasters score in the top of intelligence tests but are not geniuses; what distinguishes them is method. First, they **Fermi-ize**: like Enrico Fermi estimating Chicago's piano tuners from population and piano-ownership guesses, they break impossible questions into tractable sub-questions and flush hidden assumptions into the open. Asked whether tests would find polonium in Yasser Arafat's remains, a superforecaster first asks: how does polonium decay, who wants the test to succeed, what must all be true?

Second, they take the **outside view** before the **inside view**: start with the **base rate** — how often do such situations generally end this way? — then adjust for the case's specifics. Most people do the reverse, diving into vivid details and anchoring on a story (Kahneman: a first estimate anchors everything after).

Third, they cultivate the **dragonfly eye**: actively hunting for perspectives that contradict their own and synthesizing them, effectively running a one-person wisdom-of-crowds — the aggregation logic behind Galton's ox-weighing crowd. The trait that predicts this best is **active open-mindedness**: treating beliefs as hypotheses to test, not treasures to guard.
readIf: Big ambiguous questions make you freeze or guess
You form a story first and check facts second
You want the single most transferable chapter in the book

chapters.title: Granularity: Percentages, Not Fate
chapters.summary:
Most minds run a three-setting dial: yes, no, and maybe. Superforecasters run a percentage scale and use it with unusual **granularity** — distinguishing 63% from 68%, working in increments of 5% or finer. This is not false precision: when Tetlock's team rounded superforecasters' estimates to the nearest 0.05 or 0.10, their Brier scores got measurably worse. The fine distinctions carried real information; ordinary forecasters lost nothing when rounded.

Probabilistic thinking means accepting two kinds of uncertainty: **epistemic** (unknown but knowable) and **aleatory** (irreducibly unknowable). It also means rejecting fate. People who agree that events 'happen for a reason' or were 'meant to be' score systematically worse; superforecasters overwhelmingly see history as contingent, one path among countless possibles.

Beware the **wrong-side-of-maybe fallacy**: judging a 70% forecast 'wrong' because the 30% outcome occurred. When analysts told Obama the odds that bin Laden was in the Abbottabad compound ranged around 30–95%, he heard 'fifty-fifty' — the maybe setting swallowing hard-won precision. Probabilities only pay off if consumers, not just forecasters, learn to read them.
readIf: You round every judgment to 'likely' or 'unlikely'
You call a forecast wrong whenever the underdog wins
You believe some outcomes are simply meant to be

chapters.title: Updating: Many Small Steps, Few Big Leaps
chapters.summary:
A good initial forecast is only the opening bid; accuracy comes from skillful revision. Superforecasters update far more often than others — and usually in small increments, nudging 60% to 65% as evidence trickles in. The GJP data show frequent, modest updating is among the strongest predictors of accuracy.

Two failure modes bracket the skill. **Underreaction** comes from commitment: once a belief is public or tied to identity, we defend it — belief perseverance, escalating commitment, motivated reasoning. Karl Popper's ideal of seeking disconfirmation loses to the ego's defense lawyers. **Overreaction** is chasing noise: the **dilution effect** shows even irrelevant information drags judgments around, and dramatic headlines trigger lurches that destroy calibration.

The balancing discipline is Bayesian in spirit if not in arithmetic: **Bayes' theorem** says new evidence should move your belief in proportion to its diagnosticity, anchored to your prior. Superforecaster Bill Flack captured it plainly — weigh how surprising the evidence would be under each hypothesis, then shift accordingly. Occasionally a genuinely diagnostic fact warrants a jump; wisdom is telling signal-rich surprises from noise. Update when facts change, by roughly as much as the facts deserve.
readIf: You defend positions harder once you've stated them publicly
Breaking news whipsaws your opinions
You want a practical, math-light Bayesian habit

chapters.title: Perpetual Beta: How Forecasters Improve
chapters.summary:
Superforecasters treat their own judgment as software in **perpetual beta** — never finished, always being patched. In the GJP data, this commitment to improvement predicted superforecaster status about three times more strongly than any rival trait, including intelligence.

The psychology behind it: Carol Dweck's **growth mindset** (ability is built, not fixed — her studies show praise for effort beats praise for talent) plus Angela Duckworth's **grit** (sustained passion and persistence). But effort alone is not enough; skill needs **practice with prompt, unambiguous feedback**. Police officers grow confident in spotting lies without growing accurate, because they rarely learn the truth — confidence inflates while skill stagnates.

Forecasting feedback is naturally corrupted by vague wording, elastic timelines, and **hindsight bias** — Baruch Fischhoff showed we misremember our past predictions to match outcomes, so we 'knew it all along' and learn nothing. The remedy is the tournament discipline: precise questions, timestamped probabilities, Brier scores, and honest **postmortems** on failures and on lucky wins alike. The loop is try, fail, analyze, adjust, try again — the same cycle as John Boyd's fighter-pilot OODA thinking, applied to belief itself.
readIf: You replay decisions long after making them
You're confident in skills you've never gotten feedback on
You want a concrete self-training loop, not a pep talk

chapters.title: Superteams: When Groups Beat Their Best Member
chapters.summary:
Groups can be smarter or dumber than their members. Irving Janis's **groupthink** analysis of the Bay of Pigs shows cohesion breeding unexamined consensus; yet in the GJP, forecasters randomly assigned to teams beat independent forecasters by 23%, and teams of superforecasters trounced prediction markets by 15–30%. Structure decides which way it goes.

Winning teams shared norms: **precision questioning** (Dennis Matthies's technique of drilling into what exactly a claim means and rests on), Andy Grove's **constructive confrontation** — challenging ideas without attacking people — and a culture of sharing, where Adam Grant's **givers** outperformed takers rather than being exploited. JFK's redesigned process after the Bay of Pigs — skepticism mandated, hierarchy suspended — produced the Cuban Missile Crisis deliberations.

Aggregation is the other lever. Scott Page's theorem that **diversity trumps ability** explains why combining different error patterns beats adding another strong-but-similar mind. GJP's best algorithm averaged forecasts weighted toward proven forecasters, then **extremized** the result (pushing 70% toward 85%) to recover the confidence lost when individuals hold private slivers of information — and that formula won the whole IARPA tournament.
readIf: Your meetings converge fast and regret slowly
You build or run forecasting, investing, or strategy teams
You wonder whether markets or committees predict better

chapters.title: Leaders, Black Swans, and the Ten Commandments
chapters.summary:
Can a leader be a doubt-embracing fox and still decide and inspire? The Prussian answer: Helmuth von Moltke's officers were trained that 'no plan survives contact with the enemy,' yet acted decisively through **Auftragstaktik** — mission command: leaders set the goal, subordinates improvise the means. The resolution is humility about the world's predictability, not about oneself: be confident you can learn, never certain you already know.

Then Taleb's challenge: if history is driven by unforecastable **black swans**, tournament accuracy on 12-month questions is trivia. Tetlock concedes real limits — predictability decays sharply with time (weather-like, roughly months, not years; Edward Lorenz's chaos work explains why), and nobody foresaw a Tunisian fruit vendor's self-immolation igniting the Arab Spring. But consequences unfold over forecastable horizons, extreme events have estimable base rates, and Kahneman's worry that **scope insensitivity** would blind forecasters to timeframes proved largely wrong in testing — superforecasters noticed.

The book closes with **Ten Commandments** for aspiring superforecasters: triage questions worth effort; Fermi-ize; balance outside and inside views; balance under- and overreaction; balance prudence and decisiveness; own your errors without hindsight excuses; and — the eleventh — don't treat commandments as commandments. Judgment beats rules; that's the point.
readIf: You must project confidence while staying honest about uncertainty
Taleb's black-swan argument made you write off forecasting
You want the whole method compressed into a checklist
